does: This paper addresses the practical challenge of identifying which training data to remove when unlearning undesirable knowledge from LLMs. Rather than assuming forget and retain sets are pre-specified, the authors recognize that unlearning is typically triggered by unwanted model outputs at inference time, requiring effective retrieval of relevant training examples. They introduce data Pareto improvement as a formal framework showing how better data retrieval expands the achievable trade-off between forgetting undesired knowledge and retaining desired capabilities. The core contribution is RASLIK, a retrieval algorithm combining permutation-projection hashing with randomized antipodal search on a linearized influence kernel. The method reduces selection variance, achieves sublinear computational complexity, and demonstrates simultaneous improvements in both retrieval quality and efficiency across multiple LLM architectures and unlearning algorithms.
connects: This work builds on prior research in machine unlearning and influence-based data selection. Earlier unlearning approaches focused on parameter optimization techniques to remove knowledge while preserving model utility, treating data retrieval as a separate or assumed problem. The paper connects to influence function literature, which estimates how training examples affect model predictions and outputs. It extends prior work on data-centric machine learning that recognizes data quality and selection as critical bottlenecks. The notion of Pareto improvement in machine learning draws from multi-objective optimization traditions. RASLIK's use of hashing and antipodal search relates to approximate nearest neighbor retrieval methods and sketching techniques from randomized algorithms, adapting these for the influence-estimation setting where exact computation is prohibitively expensive.
enables: This work enables more practical and effective LLM unlearning systems by tackling the critical but underexplored data retrieval bottleneck. It opens directions for adaptive unlearning pipelines that can dynamically identify and remove problematic training data after deployment without requiring manual specification of undesired examples. The data Pareto improvement framework suggests future work on characterizing optimal data retrieval strategies for different unlearning algorithms and model architectures. The efficient sublinear retrieval capability could enable real-time unlearning in deployed systems. Beyond unlearning, the influence-kernel-based retrieval approach may apply to other data-centric problems like data valuation, anomaly detection, and targeted model debugging. The work also motivates investigation of tighter connections between retrieval quality and downstream unlearning performance across the forgetting-retention trade-off frontier.
